question_text: Find the unit vector in the direction of sum of vectors {tex}\vec{a}=2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}=2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} .
question_html: Find the unit vector in the direction of sum of vectors {tex}\vec{a}=2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}=2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex}.
solution_text: We have, {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} Since, unit vector is needed to be found in the direction of the sum of vectors {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} . So, add vectors {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} . Let, {tex}\vec{\mathrm{c}}=\vec{\mathrm{a}}+\vec{\mathrm{b}}{/tex} Substituting the values of vectors {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} . {tex}\Rightarrow \vec{\mathrm{c}}=(2 \hat{1}-\hat{\jmath}+\hat{\mathrm{k}})+(2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \vec{\mathrm{c}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \vec{\mathrm{c}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \vec{\mathrm{c}}=2 \hat{i}+\hat{\jmath}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} We know that, a unit vector in a normed vector space is a vector (often a spatial vector) of length 1. To find a unit vector with the same direction as a given vector, we divide by the magnitude of the vector. For finding unit vector, we have the formula: {tex}\widehat{c}=\frac{\vec{c}}{|\vec{c}|}{/tex} Substitute the value of {tex}\vec c{/tex} . {tex}\Rightarrow \widehat{c}=\frac{2 \hat{\imath}+\hat{\jmath}+2 \hat{k}}{|2 \hat{\imath}+\hat{\jmath}+2 \hat{k}|}{/tex} Here, {tex}|2 \hat{\imath}+\hat{\jmath}+2 \hat{k}|=\sqrt{2^{2}+1^{2}+2^{2}}{/tex} . {tex}\Rightarrow \hat{c}=\frac{2 \hat{\imath}+\hat{\jmath}+2 \hat{k}}{\sqrt{2^{2}+1^{2}+2^{2}}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \widehat{c}=\frac{2 \hat{\imath}+\hat{\jmath}+2 \hat{k}}{\sqrt{4+1+4}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \widehat{c}=\frac{2 \hat{\imath}+\hat{\jmath}+2 \hat{k}}{\sqrt{9}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \widehat{c}=\frac{2 \hat{\imath}+\hat{\jmath}+2 \hat{k}}{3}{/tex} .. Thus, unit vector in the direction of sum of vectors {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} is {tex}\frac{2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+2 \tilde{k}}{3}{/tex} .
solution_html:
We have, 
{tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}
Since, unit vector is needed to be found in the direction of the sum of vectors {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex}.
So, add vectors {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex}.
Let, 
{tex}\vec{\mathrm{c}}=\vec{\mathrm{a}}+\vec{\mathrm{b}}{/tex}
Substituting the values of vectors {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex}.
{tex}\Rightarrow \vec{\mathrm{c}}=(2 \hat{1}-\hat{\jmath}+\hat{\mathrm{k}})+(2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \vec{\mathrm{c}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \vec{\mathrm{c}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \vec{\mathrm{c}}=2 \hat{i}+\hat{\jmath}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}

We know that, a unit vector in a normed vector space is a vector (often a spatial vector) of length 1.
To find a unit vector with the same direction as a given vector, we divide by the magnitude of the vector.
For finding unit vector, we have the formula:
{tex}\widehat{c}=\frac{\vec{c}}{|\vec{c}|}{/tex}
Substitute the value of {tex}\vec c{/tex}.
{tex}\Rightarrow \widehat{c}=\frac{2 \hat{\imath}+\hat{\jmath}+2 \hat{k}}{|2 \hat{\imath}+\hat{\jmath}+2 \hat{k}|}{/tex}
Here, {tex}|2 \hat{\imath}+\hat{\jmath}+2 \hat{k}|=\sqrt{2^{2}+1^{2}+2^{2}}{/tex}.
{tex}\Rightarrow \hat{c}=\frac{2 \hat{\imath}+\hat{\jmath}+2 \hat{k}}{\sqrt{2^{2}+1^{2}+2^{2}}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \widehat{c}=\frac{2 \hat{\imath}+\hat{\jmath}+2 \hat{k}}{\sqrt{4+1+4}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \widehat{c}=\frac{2 \hat{\imath}+\hat{\jmath}+2 \hat{k}}{\sqrt{9}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \widehat{c}=\frac{2 \hat{\imath}+\hat{\jmath}+2 \hat{k}}{3}{/tex}..
Thus, unit vector in the direction of sum of vectors {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} is {tex}\frac{2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+2 \tilde{k}}{3}{/tex}.

question_text: If {tex}\vec{a}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}=2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}{/tex} , find the unit vector in the direction of 6 {tex}\vec b{/tex} .
question_html: If {tex}\vec{a}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}=2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}{/tex}, find the unit vector in the direction of 6{tex}\vec b{/tex}.
solution_text: We have, {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} {tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} We need to find the unit vector in the direction of 6 {tex}\vec b{/tex} . First, let us calculate 6 {tex}\vec b{/tex} . As we have, {tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} Multiply it by 6 on both sides. {tex}\Rightarrow 6 \vec{\mathrm{b}}=6(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}){/tex} For finding unit vector, we have the formula: {tex}6 \hat{\mathrm{b}}=\frac{6 \vec{\mathrm{b}}}{|6 \vec{\mathrm{b}}|}{/tex} Now we know the value of 6 {tex}\vec b{/tex} , so just substitute the value in the above equation. {tex}\Rightarrow 6 \hat{\mathrm{b}}=\frac{12 \hat{1}+6 \hat{\jmath}-12 \hat{\mathrm{k}}}{|12 \hat{1}+6 \hat{\jmath}-12 \hat{\mathrm{k}}|}{/tex} Here, {tex}|12 \hat{\imath}+6 \hat{\jmath}-12 \hat{\mathrm{k}}|=\sqrt{12^{2}+6^{2}+(-12)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 6 \hat{\mathrm{b}}=\frac{12 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-12 \hat{\mathrm{k}}}{\sqrt{144+36+144}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 6 \hat{\mathrm{b}}=\frac{12 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-12 \hat{\mathrm{k}}}{\sqrt{324}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 6 \hat{\mathrm{b}}=\frac{12 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-12 \hat{\mathrm{k}}}{18}{/tex} Let us simplify. {tex}\Rightarrow 6 \widehat{\mathrm{b}}=\frac{6(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{k}})}{18}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 6 \widehat{\mathrm{b}}=\frac{2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}}{3}{/tex} Thus, unit vector in the direction of {tex}6\vec b{/tex} is {tex}\frac{2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}}{3}{/tex} .
solution_html:
We have,
{tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}
We need to find the unit vector in the direction of 6{tex}\vec b{/tex}.
First, let us calculate 6{tex}\vec b{/tex}.
As we have,
{tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}
Multiply it by 6 on both sides.
{tex}\Rightarrow 6 \vec{\mathrm{b}}=6(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}){/tex}
For finding unit vector, we have the formula:
{tex}6 \hat{\mathrm{b}}=\frac{6 \vec{\mathrm{b}}}{|6 \vec{\mathrm{b}}|}{/tex}
Now we know the value of 6{tex}\vec b{/tex}, so just substitute the value in the above equation.
{tex}\Rightarrow 6 \hat{\mathrm{b}}=\frac{12 \hat{1}+6 \hat{\jmath}-12 \hat{\mathrm{k}}}{|12 \hat{1}+6 \hat{\jmath}-12 \hat{\mathrm{k}}|}{/tex}
Here, {tex}|12 \hat{\imath}+6 \hat{\jmath}-12 \hat{\mathrm{k}}|=\sqrt{12^{2}+6^{2}+(-12)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 6 \hat{\mathrm{b}}=\frac{12 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-12 \hat{\mathrm{k}}}{\sqrt{144+36+144}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 6 \hat{\mathrm{b}}=\frac{12 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-12 \hat{\mathrm{k}}}{\sqrt{324}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 6 \hat{\mathrm{b}}=\frac{12 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-12 \hat{\mathrm{k}}}{18}{/tex}
Let us simplify.
{tex}\Rightarrow 6 \widehat{\mathrm{b}}=\frac{6(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{k}})}{18}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 6 \widehat{\mathrm{b}}=\frac{2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}}{3}{/tex}
Thus, unit vector in the direction of {tex}6\vec b{/tex} is {tex}\frac{2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}}{3}{/tex}.

question_text: If {tex}\vec{a}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}=2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}{/tex} , find the unit vector in the direction of {tex}2\vec a - \vec b{/tex} .
question_html: If {tex}\vec{a}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}=2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}{/tex}, find the unit vector in the direction of {tex}2\vec a - \vec b{/tex}.
solution_text: We need to find the unit vector in the direction of {tex}2\vec a - \vec b{/tex} . First, let us calculate {tex}2\vec a - \vec b{/tex} . As we have, {tex}\vec{a}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} ...(a) {tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}}=2 \hat{i}+\hat{\jmath}-2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} ...(b) Then multiply equation (a) by 2 on both sides, {tex}2 \vec{\mathrm{a}}=2(\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}){/tex} We can easily multiply vector by a scalar by multiplying similar components, that is, vector’s magnitude by the scalar’s magnitude. {tex}\Rightarrow 2 \vec{\mathrm{a}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} Subtract (b) from (c). We get, {tex}2 \vec{\mathrm{a}}-\vec{\mathrm{b}}=(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{k}})-(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2 \vec{\mathrm{a}}-\vec{\mathrm{b}}=2 \hat{i}-2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{k}}+ 2\vec k{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2 \vec{\mathrm{a}}-\vec{\mathrm{b}}=\hat{\mathrm{j}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} For finding unit vector, we have the formula: {tex}2 \hat{a}-\widehat{b}=\frac{2 \vec{a}-\vec{b}}{|2 \vec{a}-\vec{b}|}{/tex} Now we know the value of {tex}2 \vec{a}-\vec{b}{/tex} , so we just need to substitute in the above equation. {tex}\Rightarrow 2 \hat{a}-\widehat{b}=\frac{\hat{\jmath}+6 \hat{k}}{|\hat{\jmath}+6 \hat{k}|}{/tex} Here, {tex}\text { }|\hat{\jmath}+6 \hat{\mathrm{k}}|=\sqrt{1^{2}+6^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2 \hat{a}-\widehat{b}=\frac{\hat{\jmath}+6 \hat{k}}{\sqrt{1^{2}+6^{2}}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2 \hat{\mathrm{a}}-\hat{\mathrm{b}}=\frac{\hat{\mathrm{j}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{k}}}{\sqrt{1+36}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2 \hat{a}-\hat{b}=\frac{\hat{\jmath}+6 \hat{k}}{\sqrt{37}}{/tex} Thus, unit vector in the direction of {tex}2\vec a - \vec b{/tex} is {tex}\frac{\hat{\jmath}+6 \hat{k}}{\sqrt{37}}{/tex} .
solution_html:
We need to find the unit vector in the direction of {tex}2\vec a - \vec b{/tex}.
First, let us calculate {tex}2\vec a - \vec b{/tex}.
As we have,
{tex}\vec{a}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} ...(a)
{tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}}=2 \hat{i}+\hat{\jmath}-2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} ...(b)
Then multiply equation (a) by 2 on both sides,
{tex}2 \vec{\mathrm{a}}=2(\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}){/tex}
We can easily multiply vector by a scalar by multiplying similar components, that is, vector’s magnitude by the scalar’s magnitude.
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 \vec{\mathrm{a}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}
Subtract (b) from (c). We get,
{tex}2 \vec{\mathrm{a}}-\vec{\mathrm{b}}=(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{k}})-(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 \vec{\mathrm{a}}-\vec{\mathrm{b}}=2 \hat{i}-2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{k}}+ 2\vec k{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 \vec{\mathrm{a}}-\vec{\mathrm{b}}=\hat{\mathrm{j}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}
For finding unit vector, we have the formula:
{tex}2 \hat{a}-\widehat{b}=\frac{2 \vec{a}-\vec{b}}{|2 \vec{a}-\vec{b}|}{/tex}
Now we know the value of {tex}2 \vec{a}-\vec{b}{/tex}, so we just need to substitute in the above equation.
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 \hat{a}-\widehat{b}=\frac{\hat{\jmath}+6 \hat{k}}{|\hat{\jmath}+6 \hat{k}|}{/tex}
 Here,{tex}\text { }|\hat{\jmath}+6 \hat{\mathrm{k}}|=\sqrt{1^{2}+6^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 \hat{a}-\widehat{b}=\frac{\hat{\jmath}+6 \hat{k}}{\sqrt{1^{2}+6^{2}}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 \hat{\mathrm{a}}-\hat{\mathrm{b}}=\frac{\hat{\mathrm{j}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{k}}}{\sqrt{1+36}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 \hat{a}-\hat{b}=\frac{\hat{\jmath}+6 \hat{k}}{\sqrt{37}}{/tex}
Thus, unit vector in the direction of {tex}2\vec a - \vec b{/tex} is {tex}\frac{\hat{\jmath}+6 \hat{k}}{\sqrt{37}}{/tex}.

question_text: Find a unit vector in the direction of {tex}\overrightarrow {PQ} {/tex} , where P and Q have co-ordinates (5, 0, 8) and (3, 3, 2) respectively.
question_html: Find a unit vector in the direction of {tex}\overrightarrow {PQ} {/tex} , where P and Q have co-ordinates (5, 0, 8) and (3, 3, 2) respectively.
solution_text: Since, the coordinates of P and Q are (5, 0, 8) and (3, 3, 2) respectively. {tex}\therefore \overrightarrow {PQ} = \overrightarrow {OQ} - \overrightarrow {OP} {/tex} {tex}= \left( {3\hat i + 3\hat j + 2\hat k} \right) - \left( {5\hat i + 0\hat j + 8\hat k} \right){/tex} {tex} = - 2\hat i + 3\hat j - 6\hat k{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Unit vector in the direction of {tex}\overrightarrow {PQ} = \frac{{\overrightarrow {PQ} }}{{\left| {\overrightarrow {PQ} } \right|}} = \frac{{ - 2\hat i + 3\hat j - 6\hat k}}{{\sqrt {{2^2} + {3^2} + {6^2}} }}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{ - 2\hat i + 3\hat j - 6\hat k}}{{\sqrt {49} }} = \frac{{ - 2\hat i + 3\hat j - 6\hat k}}{7}{/tex}
solution_html:
Since, the coordinates of P and Q are (5, 0, 8) and (3, 3, 2) respectively.

{tex}\therefore \overrightarrow {PQ} = \overrightarrow {OQ} - \overrightarrow {OP} {/tex}

{tex}= \left( {3\hat i + 3\hat j + 2\hat k} \right) - \left( {5\hat i + 0\hat j + 8\hat k} \right){/tex}

{tex} = - 2\hat i + 3\hat j - 6\hat k{/tex}

{tex}\therefore{/tex} Unit vector in the direction of {tex}\overrightarrow {PQ} = \frac{{\overrightarrow {PQ} }}{{\left| {\overrightarrow {PQ} } \right|}} = \frac{{ - 2\hat i + 3\hat j - 6\hat k}}{{\sqrt {{2^2} + {3^2} + {6^2}} }}{/tex}

{tex}= \frac{{ - 2\hat i + 3\hat j - 6\hat k}}{{\sqrt {49} }} = \frac{{ - 2\hat i + 3\hat j - 6\hat k}}{7}{/tex}

question_text: If {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} are the position vectors of A and B respectively, find the position vector of a point C in BA produced such that {tex}\vec {BC}{/tex} = 1.5 {tex}\vec {BA}{/tex} .
question_html: If  {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex}  are the position vectors of A and B respectively, find the position vector of a point C in BA produced such that {tex}\vec {BC}{/tex} = 1.5 {tex}\vec {BA}{/tex}.
solution_text: Since, {tex}\overrightarrow {OA} = \vec a{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow {OB} = \vec b{/tex} {tex}\therefore \overrightarrow {BA} = \overrightarrow {OA} - \overrightarrow {OB} = \vec a - \vec b{/tex} And {tex}1.5\overrightarrow {BA} = 1.5\left( {\vec a - \vec b} \right){/tex} Since, {tex}\overrightarrow {BC} = 1.5\overrightarrow {BA} = 1.5(\vec a - \vec b){/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow {OC} - \overrightarrow {OB} = 1.5\vec a - 1.5\vec b{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow {OC} = 1.5\vec a - 1.5\vec b + \vec b\,\,\left[ {\because \overrightarrow {OB} = \vec b} \right]{/tex} {tex}= 1.5\vec a - 0.5\vec b{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{3\vec a - \vec b}}{2}{/tex} Graphically, explanation of the above solution is given below
solution_html:
Since, {tex}\overrightarrow {OA} = \vec a{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow {OB} = \vec b{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore \overrightarrow {BA} = \overrightarrow {OA} - \overrightarrow {OB} = \vec a - \vec b{/tex} 
And {tex}1.5\overrightarrow {BA} = 1.5\left( {\vec a - \vec b} \right){/tex} 
Since, {tex}\overrightarrow {BC} = 1.5\overrightarrow {BA} = 1.5(\vec a - \vec b){/tex} 
{tex}\overrightarrow {OC} - \overrightarrow {OB} = 1.5\vec a - 1.5\vec b{/tex} 
{tex}\overrightarrow {OC} = 1.5\vec a - 1.5\vec b + \vec b\,\,\left[ {\because \overrightarrow {OB} = \vec b} \right]{/tex}
{tex}= 1.5\vec a - 0.5\vec b{/tex}  
{tex} = \frac{{3\vec a - \vec b}}{2}{/tex}

question_text: Using vectors, find the value of k such that the points (k, -10, 3), (1, -1, 3) and (3, 5, 3) are collinear.
question_html: Using vectors, find the value of k such that the points (k, -10, 3), (1, -1, 3) and (3, 5, 3) are collinear.
solution_text: Let the points are A(k, -10, 3), B(1, -1, 3) and C(3, 5, 3) {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\vec{A B}=\vec{O B}-\vec{O A}{/tex} [By Traingle Law of vectors] {tex}=(\hat{i}-\hat{j}+3 \hat{k})-(k \hat{i}-10 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k})=(1-k) \hat{i}+9 \hat{j}{/tex} and {tex}\vec {B C}=\vec{O C}-\vec{O B}{/tex} {tex}=(3 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k})-(\hat{i}-\hat{j}+3 \hat{k})=2 \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}{/tex} If point A, B, C are collinear then there exists some scalar {tex}\lambda{/tex} such that {tex}\Rightarrow \vec{A B}=\lambda \vec{B C}{/tex} {tex}(1-k) \hat{i}+9 \hat{j}=\lambda(2 \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}){/tex} Comparing coefficients we get 1 - k = 2 {tex}\lambda{/tex} and 9 = 6 {tex}\lambda{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} 3 - 3k = 9 {tex}\therefore{/tex} k = -2.
solution_html:
Let the points are A(k, -10, 3), B(1, -1, 3) and C(3, 5, 3)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\vec{A B}=\vec{O B}-\vec{O A}{/tex}  [By Traingle Law of vectors]
{tex}=(\hat{i}-\hat{j}+3 \hat{k})-(k \hat{i}-10 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k})=(1-k) \hat{i}+9 \hat{j}{/tex}
and {tex}\vec {B C}=\vec{O C}-\vec{O B}{/tex}
{tex}=(3 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k})-(\hat{i}-\hat{j}+3 \hat{k})=2 \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}{/tex}
If point A, B, C are collinear then there exists some scalar {tex}\lambda{/tex} such that
{tex}\Rightarrow \vec{A B}=\lambda \vec{B C}{/tex}
{tex}(1-k) \hat{i}+9 \hat{j}=\lambda(2 \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}){/tex}
Comparing coefficients we get 1 - k = 2 {tex}\lambda{/tex} and 9 = 6{tex}\lambda{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 3 - 3k = 9
{tex}\therefore{/tex} k = -2.

question_text: A vector {tex}\vec r{/tex} is inclined at equal angles to the three axes. If the magnitude of {tex}\vec r{/tex} is {tex}2\sqrt 3 {/tex} units, find {tex}\vec r{/tex}
question_html: A vector {tex}\vec r{/tex} is inclined at equal angles to the three axes. If the magnitude of {tex}\vec r{/tex} is {tex}2\sqrt 3 {/tex} units, find {tex}\vec r{/tex}
solution_text: We have, {tex}\left| {\vec r} \right| = 2\sqrt 3 {/tex} Since, {tex}{\vec r}{/tex} is equally inclined to the three axes, {tex}{/tex} so direction cosines of the unit vector {tex}{\vec r}{/tex} will be same i.e.,l = m = n. We know that, l 2 + m 2 + n 2 = 1 {tex} \Rightarrow {l^2} + {l^2} + {l^2} = 1{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {l^2} = \frac{1}{3}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow l = \pm \left( {\frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}} \right){/tex} So, {tex}\hat r = \pm \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}\hat i \pm \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}\hat j \pm \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}\hat k{/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec r = \hat r\left| {\vec r} \right|\,\,\left[ {\because \vec r = \frac{{\vec r}}{{\left| {\vec r} \right|}}} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \left[ { \pm \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}\hat i \pm \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}\hat j \pm \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}\hat k} \right]2\sqrt 3 \,\,\left[ {\because \left| r \right| = 2\sqrt 3 } \right]{/tex} {tex}= \pm 2\hat i \pm 2\hat j \pm 2\hat k = \pm 2\left( {\hat i + \hat j + \hat k} \right){/tex}
solution_html:
We have, {tex}\left| {\vec r} \right| = 2\sqrt 3 {/tex}
Since, {tex}{\vec r}{/tex} is equally inclined to the three axes, {tex}{/tex} so direction cosines of the unit vector {tex}{\vec r}{/tex} will be same i.e.,l = m = n.
We know that,
l2 + m2 + n2 = 1
{tex} \Rightarrow {l^2} + {l^2} + {l^2} = 1{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow {l^2} = \frac{1}{3}{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow l = \pm \left( {\frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}} \right){/tex}
So, {tex}\hat r = \pm \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}\hat i \pm \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}\hat j \pm \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}\hat k{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \vec r = \hat r\left| {\vec r} \right|\,\,\left[ {\because \vec r = \frac{{\vec r}}{{\left| {\vec r} \right|}}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \left[ { \pm \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}\hat i \pm \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}\hat j \pm \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}\hat k} \right]2\sqrt 3 \,\,\left[ {\because \left| r \right| = 2\sqrt 3 } \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \pm 2\hat i \pm 2\hat j \pm 2\hat k = \pm 2\left( {\hat i + \hat j + \hat k} \right){/tex}

question_text: A vector {tex}\vec r{/tex} has magnitude 14 units and direction ratios 2, 3, -6. Find the direction cosines and components of {tex}\vec r{/tex} , given that {tex}\vec r{/tex} makes an acute angle with x-axis.
question_html: A vector {tex}\vec r{/tex} has magnitude 14 units and direction ratios 2, 3, -6. Find the direction cosines and components of {tex}\vec r{/tex}, given that {tex}\vec r{/tex} makes an acute angle with x-axis.
solution_text: Here, {tex}\left| {\vec r} \right| = 14,\vec a = 2k,\vec b = 3k{/tex} and {tex}\vec c = - 6k{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Direction cosines l, m and n are {tex}l = \frac{{\vec a}}{{\left| {\vec r} \right|}} = \frac{{2k}}{{14}} = \frac{k}{7}{/tex} {tex}m = \frac{{\vec b}}{{\left| {\vec r} \right|}} = \frac{{3k}}{{14}}{/tex} And {tex}n = \frac{{\vec c}}{{\left| {\vec r} \right|}} = \frac{{ - 6k}}{{14}} = \frac{{ - 3k}}{7}{/tex} Also, we know that {tex}{l^2} + {m^2} + {n^2} = 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{{k^2}}}{{49}} + \frac{{9{k^2}}}{{196}} + \frac{{9{k^2}}}{{49}} = 1{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{4{k^2} + 9{k^2} + 36{k^2}}}{{196}} = 1{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {k^2} = \frac{{196}}{{49}} = 4{/tex} {tex} {k} = \pm 2{/tex} So, the direction cosines (l, m, n) are {tex}\frac{2}{7},\frac{3}{7}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{{ - 6}}{7}{/tex} [since, {tex}\vec r{/tex} makes an acute angle with X-axis] {tex}Now, \vec r = \hat r.\left| {\vec r} \right|{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \vec r = \left( {l\hat i + m\hat j + n\hat k} \right)\left| {\vec r} \right|{/tex} {tex}= \left( {\frac{{ 2}}{7}\hat i + \frac{3}{7}\hat j - \frac{6}{7}\hat k} \right).14{/tex} {tex} = 4\hat i + 6\hat j - 12\hat k{/tex}
solution_html:
Here, {tex}\left| {\vec r} \right| = 14,\vec a = 2k,\vec b = 3k{/tex} and {tex}\vec c = - 6k{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Direction cosines l, m and n are
{tex}l = \frac{{\vec a}}{{\left| {\vec r} \right|}} = \frac{{2k}}{{14}} = \frac{k}{7}{/tex}
{tex}m = \frac{{\vec b}}{{\left| {\vec r} \right|}} = \frac{{3k}}{{14}}{/tex}
And {tex}n = \frac{{\vec c}}{{\left| {\vec r} \right|}} = \frac{{ - 6k}}{{14}} = \frac{{ - 3k}}{7}{/tex}
Also, we know that
{tex}{l^2} + {m^2} + {n^2} = 1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{{k^2}}}{{49}} + \frac{{9{k^2}}}{{196}} + \frac{{9{k^2}}}{{49}} = 1{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{4{k^2} + 9{k^2} + 36{k^2}}}{{196}} = 1{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow {k^2} = \frac{{196}}{{49}} = 4{/tex}
{tex} {k} = \pm 2{/tex}
So, the direction cosines (l, m, n) are {tex}\frac{2}{7},\frac{3}{7}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{{ - 6}}{7}{/tex}
[since, {tex}\vec r{/tex} makes an acute angle with X-axis]
{tex}Now, \vec r = \hat r.\left| {\vec r} \right|{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \vec r = \left( {l\hat i + m\hat j + n\hat k} \right)\left| {\vec r} \right|{/tex}
{tex}= \left( {\frac{{ 2}}{7}\hat i + \frac{3}{7}\hat j - \frac{6}{7}\hat k} \right).14{/tex}
{tex} = 4\hat i + 6\hat j - 12\hat k{/tex}

question_text: Find a vector of magnitude 6, which is perpendicular to both the vectors {tex}2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}4 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+3 \hat{k}{/tex} .
question_html: Find a vector of magnitude 6, which is perpendicular to both the vectors {tex}2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}4 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+3 \hat{k}{/tex}.
solution_text: Let the vectors be {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} , such that {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} {tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}}=4 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} We need to find a vector perpendicular to both the vectors {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} . Any vector perpendicular to both {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} can be given as, {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} {\hat{i}} & {\hat{\mathrm{j}}} & {\hat{\mathrm{k}}} \\ {2} & {-1} & {2} \\ {4} & {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \vec{\mathrm{a}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}}{/tex} = {tex}\hat i{/tex} ((-1)(3) - (2) (-1) - {tex}\vec j{/tex} ((2)(3) - (2)(4)) + {tex}\vec k{/tex} ((2)(-1) - (-1)(4)) {tex}\Rightarrow \vec{\mathrm{a}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}}=-\hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} Let {tex}\vec{\mathrm{r}}=-\hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} As we know, a unit vector in a normed vector space is a vector (often a spatial vector) of length 1. So, A vector of magnitude 6 in the direction of {tex}\vec r{/tex} is given by, {tex}\text { vector }=6 \times \frac{\vec{\mathrm{r}}}{|\vec{\mathrm{r}}|}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \text { vector }=6 \times \frac{-\hat{\imath}+2 \hat{\jmath}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}}{|-\hat{\imath}+2 \hat{\jmath}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}|}{/tex} Where, {tex}|-\hat{\mathbf{i}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{k}}|=\sqrt{(-1)^{2}+(2)^{2}+(2)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \text { vector }=6 \times \frac{-\hat{1}+2 \hat{\jmath}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}}{\sqrt{(-1)^{2}+(2)^{2}+(2)^{2}}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \text { vector }=6 \times \frac{(-\hat{1}+2 \hat{\jmath}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}})}{\sqrt{1+4+4}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \text { vector }=6 \times \frac{-\hat{\imath}+2 \hat{\jmath}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}}{\sqrt{9}}`{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \text { vector }=2 \times(-\hat{i}+2 \hat{\jmath}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \text { vector }=-2 \hat{i}+4 \hat{\jmath}+4 \hat{k}{/tex} Thus, required vector is {tex}-2 \hat{i}+4 \hat{\jmath}+4 \hat{k}{/tex} .
solution_html:
Let the vectors be {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex}, such that
{tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}}=4 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}
We need to find a vector perpendicular to both the vectors {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex}.
Any vector perpendicular to both {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} can be given as,
{tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} {\hat{i}} & {\hat{\mathrm{j}}} & {\hat{\mathrm{k}}} \\ {2} & {-1} & {2} \\ {4} & {-1} & {3} \end{array}\right|{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \vec{\mathrm{a}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}}{/tex} = {tex}\hat i{/tex}((-1)(3) - (2) (-1) - {tex}\vec j{/tex}((2)(3) - (2)(4)) + {tex}\vec k{/tex} ((2)(-1) - (-1)(4))
{tex}\Rightarrow \vec{\mathrm{a}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}}=-\hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}
Let
{tex}\vec{\mathrm{r}}=-\hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}
As we know, a unit vector in a normed vector space is a vector (often a spatial vector) of length 1.
So,
A vector of magnitude 6 in the direction of {tex}\vec r{/tex} is given by,
{tex}\text { vector }=6 \times \frac{\vec{\mathrm{r}}}{|\vec{\mathrm{r}}|}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \text { vector }=6 \times \frac{-\hat{\imath}+2 \hat{\jmath}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}}{|-\hat{\imath}+2 \hat{\jmath}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}|}{/tex}
Where, {tex}|-\hat{\mathbf{i}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{k}}|=\sqrt{(-1)^{2}+(2)^{2}+(2)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \text { vector }=6 \times \frac{-\hat{1}+2 \hat{\jmath}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}}{\sqrt{(-1)^{2}+(2)^{2}+(2)^{2}}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \text { vector }=6 \times \frac{(-\hat{1}+2 \hat{\jmath}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}})}{\sqrt{1+4+4}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \text { vector }=6 \times \frac{-\hat{\imath}+2 \hat{\jmath}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}}{\sqrt{9}}`{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \text { vector }=2 \times(-\hat{i}+2 \hat{\jmath}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \text { vector }=-2 \hat{i}+4 \hat{\jmath}+4 \hat{k}{/tex}
Thus, required vector is {tex}-2 \hat{i}+4 \hat{\jmath}+4 \hat{k}{/tex}.

question_text: Find the angle between the vector {tex}2\hat i - \hat j + \hat k{/tex} and {tex}3\hat i + 4\hat j - \hat k{/tex}
question_html: Find the angle between the vector {tex}2\hat i - \hat j + \hat k{/tex} and {tex}3\hat i + 4\hat j - \hat k{/tex}
solution_text: Let {tex}\vec a = 2\hat i - \hat j - \hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec b = 3\hat i + 4\hat j - \hat k{/tex} We know that, angle between two vectors {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} is given by {tex}\cos \theta = \frac{{\vec a.\vec b}}{{\left| {\vec a} \right|\left| {\vec b} \right|}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{\left( {2i - j + k} \right)\left( {3i + 4j - k} \right)}}{{\sqrt {4 + 1 + 1} \sqrt {9 + 16 + 1} }}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{6 - 4 - 1}}{{\sqrt 6 \sqrt {26} }} = \frac{1}{{2\sqrt {39} }}{/tex} {tex}\theta = {\cos ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{1}{{2\sqrt {39} }}} \right){/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}\vec a = 2\hat i - \hat j - \hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec b = 3\hat i + 4\hat j - \hat k{/tex}

We know that, angle between two vectors {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} is given by

{tex}\cos \theta = \frac{{\vec a.\vec b}}{{\left| {\vec a} \right|\left| {\vec b} \right|}}{/tex}

{tex}= \frac{{\left( {2i - j + k} \right)\left( {3i + 4j - k} \right)}}{{\sqrt {4 + 1 + 1} \sqrt {9 + 16 + 1} }}{/tex}

{tex}= \frac{{6 - 4 - 1}}{{\sqrt 6 \sqrt {26} }} = \frac{1}{{2\sqrt {39} }}{/tex}

{tex}\theta = {\cos ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{1}{{2\sqrt {39} }}} \right){/tex}

question_text: If {tex}\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}=0{/tex} , show that {tex}a \times \vec{b}=\vec{b} \times \vec{c}=\vec{c} \times \vec{a}{/tex} . Interpret the result geometrically?
question_html: If {tex}\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}=0{/tex} , show that {tex}a \times \vec{b}=\vec{b} \times \vec{c}=\vec{c} \times \vec{a}{/tex} . Interpret the result geometrically?
solution_text: Since, {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}}+\vec{\mathrm{b}}+\vec{\mathrm{c}}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\vec{b}=-\vec{c}-\vec{a}{/tex} Now, {tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}=\vec{a} \times(-\vec{c}-\vec{a}){/tex} {tex}=\vec{a} \times -\vec{c}-\vec{a} \times \vec{a}=\vec{a} \times -\vec{c}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}=\vec{c} \times \vec{a}{/tex} ...(i) Also, {tex}\bar{b} \times \vec{c}=(-\vec{c}-\vec{a}) \times \vec{c}{/tex} {tex}=-\vec{c} \times \vec{c}-\vec{a} \times \vec{c}=-\vec{a} \times \vec{c}{/tex} {tex}=-\vec{c} \times \vec{c}-\vec{a} \times \vec{c}=-\vec{a} \times \vec{c} = \vec{c} \times \vec{a}{/tex} ...(ii) From (i) and (ii) {tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}=\vec{b} \times \vec{c}=\vec{c} \times \vec{a}{/tex} Geometrical interpretation of the result If ABCD is a parallelogram such that {tex}\vec{A B}{/tex} = {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{A D}{/tex} = {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} and these adjacent sides are making angle {tex}\theta{/tex} between each other, then Area of the parallelogram {tex}A B C D=|\vec{a}||\vec{b}||\sin \theta=| \vec{a} \times \vec{b} |{/tex} since parallelogram on the same base and between the same parallels are equal we have {tex}|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|=|\vec{a} \times \vec{c}|=|\vec{b} \times \vec{c}|{/tex} This also implies that, {tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}=\vec{a} \times \vec{c}=\vec{b} \times \vec{c}{/tex} So, the area of the parallelograms formed by taking any two sides represented by {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} , {tex}\vec b{/tex} and {tex}\vec c{/tex} as adjacent are equal.
solution_html:
Since, {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}}+\vec{\mathrm{b}}+\vec{\mathrm{c}}=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\vec{b}=-\vec{c}-\vec{a}{/tex}
Now, {tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}=\vec{a} \times(-\vec{c}-\vec{a}){/tex}
{tex}=\vec{a} \times -\vec{c}-\vec{a} \times \vec{a}=\vec{a} \times -\vec{c}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}=\vec{c} \times \vec{a}{/tex} ...(i)
Also, {tex}\bar{b} \times \vec{c}=(-\vec{c}-\vec{a}) \times \vec{c}{/tex}
{tex}=-\vec{c} \times \vec{c}-\vec{a} \times \vec{c}=-\vec{a} \times \vec{c}{/tex}
{tex}=-\vec{c} \times \vec{c}-\vec{a} \times \vec{c}=-\vec{a} \times \vec{c} = \vec{c} \times \vec{a}{/tex} ...(ii)
From (i) and (ii) 
{tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}=\vec{b} \times \vec{c}=\vec{c} \times \vec{a}{/tex}
Geometrical interpretation of the result
If ABCD is a parallelogram such that {tex}\vec{A B}{/tex} = {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{A D}{/tex} = {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} and these adjacent sides are making angle {tex}\theta{/tex} between each other, then Area of the parallelogram {tex}A B C D=|\vec{a}||\vec{b}||\sin \theta=| \vec{a} \times \vec{b} |{/tex}
since parallelogram on the same base and between the same parallels are equal

we have {tex}|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|=|\vec{a} \times \vec{c}|=|\vec{b} \times \vec{c}|{/tex}
This also implies that, {tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}=\vec{a} \times \vec{c}=\vec{b} \times \vec{c}{/tex}
So, the area of the parallelograms formed by taking any two sides represented by {tex}\vec{a}{/tex}, {tex}\vec b{/tex} and {tex}\vec c{/tex} as adjacent are equal.

question_text: Find the sine of the angle between the vectors {tex}\vec a = 3\hat i + \hat j + 2\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec b = 2\hat i - 2\hat j + 4\hat k{/tex}
question_html: Find the sine of the angle between the vectors {tex}\vec a = 3\hat i + \hat j + 2\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec b = 2\hat i - 2\hat j + 4\hat k{/tex}
solution_text: Here, a 1 = 3, a 2 = 1, a 3 = 2 and b 1 = 2, b 2 = -2, b 3 = 4 We know that, {tex}\cos \theta = \frac{{{a_1}{b_1} + {a_2}{b_2} + {a_3}{b_3}}}{{\sqrt {a_1^2 + a_2^2 + a_3^2} \sqrt {b_1^2 + b_2^2 + b_3^2} }}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{3 \times 2 + 1 \times \left( { - 2} \right) + 2 \times 4}}{{\sqrt {{3^2} + {1^2} + {2^2}\sqrt {{2^2} + {{\left( { - 2} \right)}^2} + {4^2}} } }}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{6 - 2 + 8}}{{\sqrt {14} \sqrt {24} }} = \frac{{12}}{{2\sqrt {14} \sqrt 6 }} = \frac{6}{{\sqrt {84} }} = \frac{6}{{2\sqrt {21} }} = \frac{3}{{\sqrt {21} }}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \sin \theta = \sqrt {1 - {{\cos }^2}\theta } {/tex} {tex}= \sqrt {1 - \frac{9}{{21}}} = \sqrt {\frac{{12}}{{21}}} = \frac{{2\sqrt 3 }}{{\sqrt 3 \sqrt 7 }} = \frac{2}{{\sqrt 7 }}{/tex}
solution_html:
Here, a1 = 3, a2 = 1, a3 = 2 and b1 = 2, b2 = -2, b3 = 4

We know that,

{tex}\cos \theta = \frac{{{a_1}{b_1} + {a_2}{b_2} + {a_3}{b_3}}}{{\sqrt {a_1^2 + a_2^2 + a_3^2} \sqrt {b_1^2 + b_2^2 + b_3^2} }}{/tex}

{tex}= \frac{{3 \times 2 + 1 \times \left( { - 2} \right) + 2 \times 4}}{{\sqrt {{3^2} + {1^2} + {2^2}\sqrt {{2^2} + {{\left( { - 2} \right)}^2} + {4^2}} } }}{/tex}

{tex}= \frac{{6 - 2 + 8}}{{\sqrt {14} \sqrt {24} }} = \frac{{12}}{{2\sqrt {14} \sqrt 6 }} = \frac{6}{{\sqrt {84} }} = \frac{6}{{2\sqrt {21} }} = \frac{3}{{\sqrt {21} }}{/tex}

{tex}\therefore \sin \theta = \sqrt {1 - {{\cos }^2}\theta } {/tex}

{tex}= \sqrt {1 - \frac{9}{{21}}} = \sqrt {\frac{{12}}{{21}}} = \frac{{2\sqrt 3 }}{{\sqrt 3 \sqrt 7 }} = \frac{2}{{\sqrt 7 }}{/tex}

question_text: If A, B, C, D are the points with position vectors {tex}\hat i +\hat j - \hat k,2\hat i - \hat j + 3\hat k,2\hat i - 3\hat k{/tex} and {tex}3\hat i - 2\hat j + \hat k{/tex} respectively, find the projection of {tex}\overrightarrow {AB} {/tex} along {tex}\overrightarrow {CD} {/tex}
question_html: If A, B, C, D are the points with position vectors {tex}\hat i +\hat j - \hat k,2\hat i - \hat j + 3\hat k,2\hat i - 3\hat k{/tex} and {tex}3\hat i - 2\hat j + \hat k{/tex} respectively, find the projection of {tex}\overrightarrow {AB} {/tex} along {tex}\overrightarrow {CD} {/tex}
solution_text: Here, {tex}\overrightarrow {OA} = \hat i + \hat j - \hat k,{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow {OB} = 2\hat i - \hat j + 3\hat k,\overrightarrow {OC} = 2\hat i - 3\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow {OD} = 3\hat i - 2\hat j + \hat k{/tex} {tex}\therefore \overrightarrow {AB} = \overrightarrow {OB} - \overrightarrow {OA} {/tex} {tex}= \left( {2 - 1} \right)\hat i + \left( { - 1 - 1} \right)\hat j + \left( {3 + 1} \right)\hat k{/tex} {tex} = \hat i - 2\hat j + 4\hat k{/tex} And {tex}\overrightarrow {CD} = \overrightarrow {OD} - \overrightarrow {OC} {/tex} {tex}= \left( {3 - 2} \right)\hat i + \left( { - 2 - 0} \right)\hat j + \left( {1 + 3} \right)\hat k{/tex} {tex} = \hat i - 2\hat j + 4\hat k{/tex} So, the projection of {tex}\overrightarrow {AB} {/tex} along {tex}\overrightarrow {CD} = \overrightarrow {AB} .\frac{{\overrightarrow {CD} }}{{\left| {\overrightarrow {CD} } \right|}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{\left( {\hat i - 2\hat j + 4\hat k} \right).\left( {\hat i - 2\hat j + 4\hat k} \right)}}{{\sqrt {{1^2} + {2^2} + {4^2}} }}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{1 + 4 + 16}}{{\sqrt {21} }} = \frac{{21}}{{\sqrt {21} }}{/tex} {tex} = \sqrt {21} {/tex} units
solution_html:
Here, {tex}\overrightarrow {OA} = \hat i + \hat j - \hat k,{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow {OB} = 2\hat i - \hat j + 3\hat k,\overrightarrow {OC} = 2\hat i - 3\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow {OD} = 3\hat i - 2\hat j + \hat k{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \overrightarrow {AB} = \overrightarrow {OB} - \overrightarrow {OA} {/tex} {tex}= \left( {2 - 1} \right)\hat i + \left( { - 1 - 1} \right)\hat j + \left( {3 + 1} \right)\hat k{/tex}
{tex} = \hat i - 2\hat j + 4\hat k{/tex}
And {tex}\overrightarrow {CD} = \overrightarrow {OD} - \overrightarrow {OC} {/tex} {tex}= \left( {3 - 2} \right)\hat i + \left( { - 2 - 0} \right)\hat j + \left( {1 + 3} \right)\hat k{/tex}
{tex} = \hat i - 2\hat j + 4\hat k{/tex}
So, the projection of {tex}\overrightarrow {AB} {/tex} along {tex}\overrightarrow {CD} = \overrightarrow {AB} .\frac{{\overrightarrow {CD} }}{{\left| {\overrightarrow {CD} } \right|}}{/tex}
{tex}= \frac{{\left( {\hat i - 2\hat j + 4\hat k} \right).\left( {\hat i - 2\hat j + 4\hat k} \right)}}{{\sqrt {{1^2} + {2^2} + {4^2}} }}{/tex}
{tex}= \frac{{1 + 4 + 16}}{{\sqrt {21} }} = \frac{{21}}{{\sqrt {21} }}{/tex}
{tex} = \sqrt {21} {/tex} units

question_text: Using vectors, find the area of the {tex}\Delta A B C,{/tex} whose vertices are A (1, 2, 3), B(2, -1, 4) and C(4, 5, -1).
question_html: Using vectors, find the area of the {tex}\Delta A B C,{/tex} whose vertices are A (1, 2, 3), B(2, -1, 4) and C(4, 5, -1).
solution_text: Given , ΔABC, whose vertices are A (1, 2, 3), B(2, -1, 4) and C(4, 5, -1). Let the position vectors of the vertices A, B and C of {tex}\Delta A B C{/tex} is {tex}\vec { O A } = \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + \hat { 3 k }{/tex} {tex} \vec { O B } = 2 \hat { i } - \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { O C } = 4 \hat { i } + 5 \hat { j } - \hat { k }{/tex} We know that , {tex}\vec { A B } = \vec { O B } - \vec { O A }{/tex} {tex}= ( 2 \hat { i } - \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k } ) - ( \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k } ){/tex} {tex}= \hat { i } - 3 \hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex} also , {tex}\vec { A C } = \vec { O C } - \vec { O A }{/tex} {tex}= ( 4 \hat { i } + 5 \hat { j } - \hat { k } ) - ( \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k } ){/tex} {tex}= ( 3 \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j } - 4 \hat { k } ){/tex} Now, {tex}\vec { A B } \times \vec { A C } = \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { \hat { i } } & { \hat { j } } & { \hat { k } } \\ { 1 } & { - 3 } & { 1 } \\ { 3 } & { 3 } & { - 4 } \end{array} \right|{/tex} {tex}= \hat { i } ( 12 - 3 ) - \hat { j } ( - 4 - 3 ) + \hat { k } ( 3 + 9 ){/tex} {tex}= 9 \hat { i } + 7 \hat { j } + 12 \hat { k }{/tex} {tex}| \vec { A B } \times \vec { A C } | = \sqrt { ( 9 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 7 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 12 ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}= \sqrt { 81 + 49 + 144 }{/tex} {tex} = \sqrt { 274 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of {tex}\Delta A B C = \frac { 1 } { 2 } | \vec { A B } \times \vec { A C } |{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sqrt { 274 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \sqrt { 274 } } { 2 } {/tex} {tex}\therefore \ Area = \frac { \sqrt { 274 } } { 2 } {/tex} sq units.
solution_html:
Given , ΔABC, whose vertices are A (1, 2, 3), B(2, -1, 4) and C(4, 5, -1).

Let the position vectors of the vertices A, B and C of {tex}\Delta A B C{/tex}  is 
{tex}\vec { O A } = \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + \hat { 3 k }{/tex}

{tex} \vec { O B } = 2 \hat { i } - \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k }{/tex} and 
{tex}\vec { O C } = 4 \hat { i } + 5 \hat { j } - \hat { k }{/tex}

We know that , {tex}\vec { A B } = \vec { O B } - \vec { O A }{/tex}
{tex}= ( 2 \hat { i } - \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k } ) - ( \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k } ){/tex}
{tex}= \hat { i } - 3 \hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex}
also ,  {tex}\vec { A C } = \vec { O C } - \vec { O A }{/tex}
{tex}= ( 4 \hat { i } + 5 \hat { j } - \hat { k } ) - ( \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k } ){/tex}
{tex}= ( 3 \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j } - 4 \hat { k } ){/tex}
Now, {tex}\vec { A B } \times \vec { A C } = \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { \hat { i } } & { \hat { j } } & { \hat { k } } \\ { 1 } & { - 3 } & { 1 } \\ { 3 } & { 3 } & { - 4 } \end{array} \right|{/tex}
{tex}= \hat { i } ( 12 - 3 ) - \hat { j } ( - 4 - 3 ) + \hat { k } ( 3 + 9 ){/tex}
{tex}= 9 \hat { i } + 7 \hat { j } + 12 \hat { k }{/tex}
{tex}| \vec { A B } \times \vec { A C } | = \sqrt { ( 9 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 7 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 12 ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex}

{tex}= \sqrt { 81 + 49 + 144 }{/tex}

{tex} = \sqrt { 274 }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of {tex}\Delta A B C = \frac { 1 } { 2 } | \vec { A B } \times \vec { A C } |{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sqrt { 274 }{/tex}

{tex}= \frac { \sqrt { 274 } } { 2 } {/tex}

{tex}\therefore \ Area = \frac { \sqrt { 274 } } { 2 } {/tex}sq units.

question_text: Using vectors, prove that the parallelogram on the same base and between the same parallels are equal in area.
question_html: Using vectors, prove that the parallelogram on the same base and between the same parallels are equal in area.
solution_text: Let ABCD and ABFE are parallelograms on the same base AB and between the same parallel line AB and DF. Let {tex}\overrightarrow {AB} = \vec a{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow {AD} = \vec b{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of parallelogram {tex}\overrightarrow {ABCD} = \vec a \times \vec b{/tex} Now, area of parallelogram of {tex}\overrightarrow {ABFE} = \overrightarrow {AB} \times \overrightarrow {AE} {/tex} {tex} = \overrightarrow {AB} \times \left( {\overrightarrow {AD} + \overrightarrow {DE} } \right){/tex} {tex} = \overrightarrow {AB} \times \left( {\vec b + k\vec a} \right){/tex} [Let {tex}\overrightarrow {DE} = k\vec a{/tex} , where k is a scalar] {tex} = \vec a \times \left( {\vec b + k\vec a} \right){/tex} {tex}= \left( {\vec a \times \vec b} \right) + \left( {\vec a \times k\vec a} \right){/tex} {tex} = \left( {\vec a \times \vec b} \right) + k\left( {\vec a \times \vec a} \right){/tex} {tex} = \left( {\vec a \times \vec b} \right)\,\,\left[ {\because \vec a \times \vec a = 0} \right]{/tex} = Area of parallelogram ABCD. Hence proved
solution_html:
Let ABCD and ABFE are parallelograms on the same base AB and between the same parallel line AB and DF.

Let {tex}\overrightarrow {AB} = \vec a{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow {AD} = \vec b{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex}Area of parallelogram {tex}\overrightarrow {ABCD} = \vec a \times \vec b{/tex} 
Now, area of parallelogram of {tex}\overrightarrow {ABFE} = \overrightarrow {AB} \times \overrightarrow {AE} {/tex} 
{tex} = \overrightarrow {AB} \times \left( {\overrightarrow {AD} + \overrightarrow {DE} } \right){/tex} 
{tex} = \overrightarrow {AB} \times \left( {\vec b + k\vec a} \right){/tex} [Let {tex}\overrightarrow {DE} = k\vec a{/tex}, where k is a scalar]
{tex} = \vec a \times \left( {\vec b + k\vec a} \right){/tex} 
{tex}= \left( {\vec a \times \vec b} \right) + \left( {\vec a \times k\vec a} \right){/tex} 
{tex} = \left( {\vec a \times \vec b} \right) + k\left( {\vec a \times \vec a} \right){/tex} 
{tex} = \left( {\vec a \times \vec b} \right)\,\,\left[ {\because \vec a \times \vec a = 0} \right]{/tex} 
= Area of parallelogram ABCD.
Hence proved

question_text: If a, b, c are the lengths of the sides opposite respectively to the angles A, B, C of a triangle ABC, show that {tex}\cos B=\frac{c^{2}+a^{2}-b^{2}}{2 a c}{/tex}
question_html: If a, b, c are the lengths of the sides opposite respectively to the angles A, B, C of a triangle ABC, show that {tex}\cos B=\frac{c^{2}+a^{2}-b^{2}}{2 a c}{/tex}
solution_text: In ABC we have {tex}\overrightarrow{B C}+\overrightarrow{C A}=\overrightarrow{B A}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \overrightarrow{B C}+\overrightarrow{C A}=-\overrightarrow{A B}{/tex} Now {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \vec{a}+\vec{b}=-\vec{c}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}=\overrightarrow{0}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \vec{a}+\vec{c}=-\vec{b}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad|\vec{a}+\vec{c}|^{2}=|\vec{b}|^{2}{/tex} Using identity in LHS ( a+ b) 2 = a 2 + b 2 + 2ab {tex}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad|\vec{a}|^{2}+|\vec{c}|^{2}+2 \vec{a} \cdot \vec{c}=|\vec{b}|^{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad|\vec{a}|^{2}+|\vec{c}|^{2}+2|\vec{a}||\vec{c}| \cos (\pi-B)=|\vec{b}|^{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad a^{2}+c^{2}+2 a c(-\cos B)=b^{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 a c (cos B)=a^{2}+c^{2}-b^{2} {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \cos B=\frac{a^{2}+c^{2}-b^{2}}{2 a c}{/tex}
solution_html:
In ABC we have
{tex}\overrightarrow{B C}+\overrightarrow{C A}=\overrightarrow{B A}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \overrightarrow{B C}+\overrightarrow{C A}=-\overrightarrow{A B}{/tex}

Now
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \vec{a}+\vec{b}=-\vec{c}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}=\overrightarrow{0}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \vec{a}+\vec{c}=-\vec{b}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad|\vec{a}+\vec{c}|^{2}=|\vec{b}|^{2}{/tex}

Using identity in LHS ( a+ b)2 = a2 + b2+ 2ab{tex}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad|\vec{a}|^{2}+|\vec{c}|^{2}+2 \vec{a} \cdot \vec{c}=|\vec{b}|^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad|\vec{a}|^{2}+|\vec{c}|^{2}+2|\vec{a}||\vec{c}| \cos (\pi-B)=|\vec{b}|^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad a^{2}+c^{2}+2 a c(-\cos B)=b^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 a c (cos B)=a^{2}+c^{2}-b^{2} {/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \cos B=\frac{a^{2}+c^{2}-b^{2}}{2 a c}{/tex}

question_text: If {tex}\vec a,\vec b,\vec c{/tex} determine the vertices of a triangle, show that {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left[ {\vec b \times \vec c + \vec c \times \vec a + \vec a \times \vec b} \right]{/tex} gives the vector area of the triangle. Hence deduce the condition that the three points {tex}\vec a,\vec b,\vec c{/tex} are collinear. Also, find the unit vector normal to the plane of the triangle.
question_html: If {tex}\vec a,\vec b,\vec c{/tex} determine the vertices of a triangle, show that {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left[ {\vec b \times \vec c + \vec c \times \vec a + \vec a \times \vec b} \right]{/tex}gives the vector area of the triangle. Hence deduce the condition that the three points {tex}\vec a,\vec b,\vec c{/tex} are collinear. Also, find the unit vector normal to the plane of the triangle.
solution_text: Since, {tex}\vec a,\vec b{/tex} and {tex}\vec c{/tex} are the vertices of a {tex}\triangle ABC{/tex} as shown. {tex}\therefore {/tex} Area of {tex}\triangle ABC = \frac{1}{2}\left| {\overrightarrow {AB} \times \overrightarrow {AC} } \right|{/tex} Now, {tex}\overrightarrow {AB} = \vec b - \vec a{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow {AC} = \vec c - \vec a{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} Area of {tex}\triangle ABC = \frac{1}{2}\left| {(\vec b - \vec a) \times (\vec c - \vec a)} \right|{/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{2}\left| {\vec b \times \vec c - \vec b \times \vec a - \vec a \times \vec c + \vec a \times \vec a} \right|{/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{2}\left| {\vec b \times \vec c + \vec a \times \vec b + \vec c \times \vec a + 0} \right|{/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{2}\left| {\vec b \times \vec c + \vec a \times \vec b + \vec c \times \vec a} \right|{/tex} ...(i) For three points to be collinear, area of the {tex}\triangle ABC{/tex} should be equal to zero. {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}\left[ {\vec b \times \vec c + \vec c \times \vec a + \vec a \times \vec b} \right] = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \vec b \times \vec c + \vec c \times \vec a + \vec a \times \vec b = 0{/tex} ...(ii) This is the required condition for collinearity of three points {tex}\vec a,\vec b{/tex} and {tex}\vec c{/tex} Let {tex}\hat n{/tex} be the unit vector normal to the plane of the {tex}\triangle ABC{/tex} {tex}\therefore \hat n = \frac{{\overrightarrow {AB} \times \overrightarrow {AC} }}{{\left| {\overrightarrow {AB} \times \overrightarrow {AC} } \right|}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{\vec a \times \vec b + \vec b \times \vec c + \vec c \times \vec a}}{{\left| {\vec a \times \vec b + \vec b \times \vec c + \vec c \times \vec a} \right|}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Since, {tex}\vec a,\vec b{/tex} and {tex}\vec c{/tex} are the vertices of a {tex}\triangle ABC{/tex} as shown.

{tex}\therefore {/tex} Area of {tex}\triangle ABC = \frac{1}{2}\left| {\overrightarrow {AB} \times \overrightarrow {AC} } \right|{/tex} 
Now, {tex}\overrightarrow {AB} = \vec b - \vec a{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow {AC} = \vec c - \vec a{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore {/tex} Area of {tex}\triangle ABC = \frac{1}{2}\left| {(\vec b - \vec a) \times (\vec c - \vec a)} \right|{/tex} 
{tex}= \frac{1}{2}\left| {\vec b \times \vec c - \vec b \times \vec a - \vec a \times \vec c + \vec a \times \vec a} \right|{/tex} 
{tex}= \frac{1}{2}\left| {\vec b \times \vec c + \vec a \times \vec b + \vec c \times \vec a + 0} \right|{/tex} 
{tex}= \frac{1}{2}\left| {\vec b \times \vec c + \vec a \times \vec b + \vec c \times \vec a} \right|{/tex}...(i)
For three points to be collinear, area of the {tex}\triangle ABC{/tex} should be equal to zero.
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}\left[ {\vec b \times \vec c + \vec c \times \vec a + \vec a \times \vec b} \right] = 0{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \vec b \times \vec c + \vec c \times \vec a + \vec a \times \vec b = 0{/tex} ...(ii)
This is the required condition for collinearity of three points {tex}\vec a,\vec b{/tex} and {tex}\vec c{/tex} 
Let {tex}\hat n{/tex} be the unit vector normal to the plane of the {tex}\triangle ABC{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore \hat n = \frac{{\overrightarrow {AB} \times \overrightarrow {AC} }}{{\left| {\overrightarrow {AB} \times \overrightarrow {AC} } \right|}}{/tex} 
{tex}= \frac{{\vec a \times \vec b + \vec b \times \vec c + \vec c \times \vec a}}{{\left| {\vec a \times \vec b + \vec b \times \vec c + \vec c \times \vec a} \right|}}{/tex}

question_text: Show that area of the parallelogram whose diagonals are represented by {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} is given by {tex}\frac{1}{2}|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|{/tex} . Also find the area of the parallelogram whose diagonals are {tex}2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-\hat{k}{/tex} .
question_html: Show that area of the parallelogram whose diagonals are represented by {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} is given by {tex}\frac{1}{2}|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|{/tex}. Also find the area of the parallelogram whose diagonals are {tex}2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-\hat{k}{/tex}.
solution_text: Let ABCD be a parallelogram such the {tex}\vec{A B}=\vec{p}, \vec{A D}=\vec{q} \Rightarrow \vec{B C}=\vec{q}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec{A C}=\vec{A B}+\vec{B C}=\vec{p}+\vec{q}=\vec{a}{/tex} .....(i) [By triangle law of vectors] And {tex}\vec{B D}=\vec{B A}+\vec{A D}=-\vec{p}+\vec{q}=\vec{b}{/tex} .....(ii) Adding (i) and (ii), we get {tex}\bar{a}+\bar{b}=2 \bar{q}{/tex} Subtracting (ii) from Eq. (i), we get {tex}\vec{a}-\vec{b}=2 \vec{p}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \vec{p}=\frac{1}{2}(\vec{a}-\vec{b}){/tex} Now, {tex}\vec{p} \times \vec{q}=\frac{1}{4}(\vec{a}-\vec{b}) \times(\vec{a}+\vec{b}){/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{4}(\bar{a} \times \bar{a}+\bar{a} \times \bar{b}-\vec{b} \times \bar{a}-\bar{b} \times \bar{b}){/tex} So, the area of a parallelogram ABCD = {tex}|\vec{p} \times \vec{q}|=\frac{1}{2}|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|{/tex} Now, area of a parallelogram, whose diagonals are {tex}2 i-j+k{/tex} and {tex}i+3 j-\dot{k}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2}|(2 i-j+k) \times(i+3 j-k)|{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} {\hat{i}} & {\hat{j}} & {\hat{k}} \\ {2} & {-1} & {1} \\ {1} & {3} & {-1} \end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2}|i(1-3)-j(-2-1)+k(6+1)|{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2}|-2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}|=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{4+9+49}=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{62} \text { sq. units }{/tex} .
solution_html:
Let ABCD be a parallelogram such the 

{tex}\vec{A B}=\vec{p}, \vec{A D}=\vec{q} \Rightarrow \vec{B C}=\vec{q}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \vec{A C}=\vec{A B}+\vec{B C}=\vec{p}+\vec{q}=\vec{a}{/tex} .....(i) [By triangle law of vectors]
And {tex}\vec{B D}=\vec{B A}+\vec{A D}=-\vec{p}+\vec{q}=\vec{b}{/tex}  .....(ii)
Adding (i) and (ii), we get
{tex}\bar{a}+\bar{b}=2 \bar{q}{/tex}
Subtracting (ii) from Eq. (i), we get
{tex}\vec{a}-\vec{b}=2 \vec{p}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \vec{p}=\frac{1}{2}(\vec{a}-\vec{b}){/tex}
Now, {tex}\vec{p} \times \vec{q}=\frac{1}{4}(\vec{a}-\vec{b}) \times(\vec{a}+\vec{b}){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{4}(\bar{a} \times \bar{a}+\bar{a} \times \bar{b}-\vec{b} \times \bar{a}-\bar{b} \times \bar{b}){/tex}
So, the area of a parallelogram ABCD = {tex}|\vec{p} \times \vec{q}|=\frac{1}{2}|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|{/tex}
Now, area of a parallelogram, whose diagonals are {tex}2 i-j+k{/tex} and {tex}i+3 j-\dot{k}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2}|(2 i-j+k) \times(i+3 j-k)|{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} {\hat{i}} & {\hat{j}} & {\hat{k}} \\ {2} & {-1} & {1} \\ {1} & {3} & {-1} \end{array}\right|{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2}|i(1-3)-j(-2-1)+k(6+1)|{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2}|-2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}|=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{4+9+49}=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{62} \text { sq. units }{/tex}.

book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: If {tex}\vec a = \hat i + \hat j + \hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec b = \hat j - \hat k{/tex} find a vector {tex}\vec c{/tex} such that {tex}\vec a \times \vec c = \vec b{/tex} and {tex}\vec a.\vec c = 3{/tex}
question_html: If {tex}\vec a = \hat i + \hat j + \hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec b = \hat j - \hat k{/tex} find a vector {tex}\vec c{/tex} such that {tex}\vec a \times \vec c = \vec b{/tex} and {tex}\vec a.\vec c = 3{/tex}
solution_text: Let {tex}\vec c = x\hat i + y\hat j + z\hat k{/tex} And {tex}\vec a = \hat i + \hat j + \hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec b = \hat j - \hat k{/tex} For {tex}\vec a \times \vec c = \vec b,{/tex} {tex}\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ 1&1&1 \\ x&y&z \end{array}} \right| = \hat j - \hat k{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \hat i\left( {z - y} \right) - \hat j\left( {z - x} \right) + \hat k\left( {y - x} \right) = \hat j - \hat k{/tex} {tex}\therefore z - y = 0{/tex} ...(i) x - z = 1 ...(ii) x - y = 1 ...(iii) Also, {tex}\vec a.\vec c = 3{/tex} {tex}\left( {\hat i + \hat j + \hat k} \right).\left( {x\hat i + y\hat j + z\hat k} \right) = 3{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow x + y + z = 3{/tex} ...(iv) On adding Eqs. (ii) and (iii), we get 2x - y - z = 2 ...(v) On solving Eqs. (iv) and (v), we get {tex}x = \frac{5}{3}{/tex} {tex}\therefore y = \frac{5}{3} - 1 = \frac{2}{3}{/tex} and {tex}z = \frac{2}{3}{/tex} Now, {tex}\vec c = \frac{5}{3}\hat i + \frac{2}{3}\hat j + \frac{2}{3}\hat k{/tex} {tex} = \frac{1}{3}\left( {5\hat i + 2\hat j + 2\hat k} \right){/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}\vec c = x\hat i + y\hat j + z\hat k{/tex}

And {tex}\vec a = \hat i + \hat j + \hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec b = \hat j - \hat k{/tex}

For {tex}\vec a \times \vec c = \vec b,{/tex}

{tex}\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ 1&1&1 \\ x&y&z \end{array}} \right| = \hat j - \hat k{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \hat i\left( {z - y} \right) - \hat j\left( {z - x} \right) + \hat k\left( {y - x} \right) = \hat j - \hat k{/tex}

{tex}\therefore z - y = 0{/tex} ...(i)

x - z = 1 ...(ii)

x - y = 1 ...(iii)

Also, {tex}\vec a.\vec c = 3{/tex}

{tex}\left( {\hat i + \hat j + \hat k} \right).\left( {x\hat i + y\hat j + z\hat k} \right) = 3{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow x + y + z = 3{/tex} ...(iv)

On adding Eqs. (ii) and (iii), we get

2x - y - z = 2 ...(v)

On solving Eqs. (iv) and (v), we get

{tex}x = \frac{5}{3}{/tex}

{tex}\therefore y = \frac{5}{3} - 1 = \frac{2}{3}{/tex} and {tex}z = \frac{2}{3}{/tex}

Now, {tex}\vec c = \frac{5}{3}\hat i + \frac{2}{3}\hat j + \frac{2}{3}\hat k{/tex}

{tex} = \frac{1}{3}\left( {5\hat i + 2\hat j + 2\hat k} \right){/tex}

question_html: The vector in the direction of the vector {tex}\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} that has magnitude 9 is

options.explanation: Let {tex}\vec{a}=\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} Unit vector in the direction of a vector {tex}\vec a{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\vec{a}}{|\vec{a}|}=\frac{\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}}{\sqrt{1^{2}+2^{2}+2^{2}}}=\frac{i-2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}}{3}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Vector in the direction of {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} with magnitude 9 {tex}=9 \cdot \frac{i-2 j+2 k}{3}=3(i-2 j+2 k){/tex} .

options.explanation: Let {tex}\vec{a}=a_{1} \hat{i}+a_{2} \hat{\jmath}+a_{3} \hat{k}{/tex} , then {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}} \times \hat{\mathrm{i}}=\left(\mathrm{a}_{1} \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\mathrm{a}_{2} \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\mathrm{a}_{3} \hat{\mathrm{k}}\right) \times \hat{\mathrm{i}}{/tex} = {tex}a_{1} \hat{\imath} \times \hat{\imath}+a_{2} \hat{\jmath} \times \hat{\imath}+a_{3} \hat{k} \times \hat{\imath}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \vec{\mathrm{a}} \times \hat{\mathrm{i}}=0-\mathrm{a}_{2} \hat{\mathrm{k}}+\mathrm{a}_{3} \hat{\mathrm{j}}(\because \hat{\mathrm{i}} \times \hat{\mathrm{i}}=0, \hat{\mathrm{j}} \times \hat{\mathrm{i}}=-\hat{\mathrm{k}}, \hat{\mathrm{k}} \times \hat{\mathrm{i}}=\hat{\mathrm{j}}){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \vec{\mathrm{a}} \times \hat{\mathrm{i}}=-\mathrm{a}_{2} \hat{\mathrm{k}}+\mathrm{a}_{3} \hat{\mathrm{j}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow|\vec{\mathrm{a}} \times \hat{\mathrm{i}}|^{2}=\mathrm{a}_{2}^{2}+\mathrm{a}_{3}^{2}{/tex} Similarly, we get {tex}\Rightarrow|\vec{\mathrm{a}} \times \hat{\mathrm{j}}|^{2}=\mathrm{a}_{1}^{2}+\mathrm{a}_{3}^{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow|\vec{\mathrm{a}} \times \hat{\mathrm{k}}|^{2}=\mathrm{a}_{1}^{2}+\mathrm{a}_{2}^{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore|\vec{\mathrm{a}} \times \hat{\mathrm{i}}|^{2}+|\vec{\mathrm{a}} \times \hat{\mathrm{j}}|^{2}+|\vec{\mathrm{a}} \times \hat{\mathrm{k}}|^{2}{/tex} = {tex}a_{2}^{2}+a_{3}^{2}+a_{1}^{2}+a_{3}^{2}+a_{1}^{2}+a_{2}^{2}{/tex} {tex}=2\left(a_{1}^{2}+a_{2}^{2}+a_{3}^{2}\right)=2|\vec{a}|^{2}(\because|\vec{a}|=\sqrt{a_{1}^{2}+a_{2}^{2}+a_{3}^{2}}){/tex} .

Then, {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{b}}=|\vec{\mathrm{a}}||\vec{\mathrm{b}}| \cos \theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 12=10 \times 2 \cos \theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \cos \theta=\frac{12}{20}=\frac{3}{5}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \sin \theta=\sqrt{1-\cos ^{2} \theta}=\sqrt{1-\left(\frac{3}{5}\right)^{2}}=\sqrt{1-\frac{9}{25}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \sin \theta=\pm \frac{4}{5}{/tex} Now, {tex}|\vec{\mathrm{a}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}}|=|\vec{\mathrm{a}}||\vec{\mathrm{b}}| \sin \theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow|\vec{\mathrm{a}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}}|=10 \times 2 \times \frac{4}{5}=16{/tex} .

Let {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}}=\lambda \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} , {tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\lambda \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{\mathrm{c}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\lambda \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} Now, {tex}\vec a{/tex} , {tex}\vec b{/tex} , {tex}\vec c{/tex} are coplanar If, {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} {\lambda} & {1} & {2} \\ {1} & {\lambda} & {-1} \\ {2} & {-1} & {\lambda} \end{array}\right|=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lambda\left(\lambda^{2}-1\right)-1(\lambda+2)+2(-1-2 \lambda)=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lambda^{3}-\lambda-\lambda-2-2-4 \lambda=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lambda^{3}-6 \lambda-4=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow(\lambda+2)\left(\lambda^{2}-2 \lambda-2\right)=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lambda=-2{/tex} and {tex}\lambda=\frac{2 \pm \sqrt{(-2)^{2}-4 \times 1 \times-2}}{2}=\frac{2 \pm \sqrt{12}}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lambda=-2{/tex} and {tex}\lambda=1 \pm \sqrt{3}{/tex} .

solution_text: Here, we have to find the value of {tex} \vec { a } \cdot \vec { b } + \vec { b } \cdot\vec { c } + \vec { c } \cdot \vec { a }.{/tex} Given, {tex} | \vec { a } | = | \vec { b } | = | \vec { c } | = 1{/tex} and {tex} \vec { a } + \vec { b } + \vec { c } = \vec { 0 }.{/tex} Now,consider, {tex} \vec { a } + \vec { b } + \vec { c } = \vec { 0 }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad ( \vec { a } + \vec { b } + \vec { c } ) ^ { 2 } = ( \vec { 0 } ) ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \vec { ( a } + \vec { b } + \vec { c } ). \ \vec { ( a } + \vec { b } + \vec { c } ) = \vec { 0 } .\vec { 0 }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \vec { a } .\vec { a } + \vec { a }.\vec { b } + \vec { a } .\vec { c } + \vec { b }. \vec { a } + \vec { b } .\vec { b } + \vec { b } \cdot \vec { c }+ \vec { a }.\vec { c }{/tex} {tex} +\vec { c }. \vec { b } + \vec { c } .\vec { c } = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow | \vec { a } | ^ { 2 } + | \vec { b } | ^ { 2 } + | \vec { c } | ^ { 2 } + 2 ( \vec { a }. \vec { b } + \vec { b }. \vec { c } + \vec { c } . \vec { c })= 0{/tex} {tex} \left[ \because \vec { x }. \vec { y } = \vec { y } .\vec { x } \text { and } \vec { x }. \vec { x } = | \vec { x } | ^ { 2 } \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 1 + 1 + 1 + 2 \vec { ( a }. \vec { b } + \vec { b }. \vec { c } + \vec { c }. \vec { a } ) = 0{/tex} {tex} [ \because | \vec { a } | = | \vec { b } | = | \vec { c } | = 1 ]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \vec { a }. \vec { b } + \vec { b }. \vec { c } + \vec { c }. \vec { a } = \frac { - 3 } { 2 }{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow | \vec { a } | ^ { 2 } + | \vec { b } | ^ { 2 } + | \vec { c } | ^ { 2 } + 2 ( \vec { a }. \vec { b } + \vec { b }. \vec { c } + \vec { c } . \vec { c })= 0{/tex}

question_html: The number of vectors of unit length perpendicular to the vectors {tex}\vec{a}=2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}=\hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex}

options.explanation: Given that, {tex}\vec{a}=2 \hat{1}+\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} , {tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}}=\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} Now, a vector which is perpendicular to both {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} is given by {tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}{/tex} = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{lll} {\hat{1}} & {\hat{{j}}} & {\hat{{k}}} \\ {2} & {1} & {2} \\ {0} & {1} & {1} \end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\vec i{/tex} (1 - 2) - {tex}\vec j{/tex} (2 - 0) + {tex}\vec j=k{/tex} (2 - 0) = {tex}-\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} Now, {tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{(-1)^{2}+(-2)^{2}+(2)^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{1+4+4}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{9}{/tex} = 3 {tex}\therefore{/tex} the required unit vector {tex}=\frac{\vec{a} \times \vec{b}}{|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|}=\frac{-i-2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}}{3}=\frac{-1}{3} \hat{\imath}-\frac{2}{3} \hat{\jmath}+\frac{2}{3} k{/tex} There are two perpendicular directions to any plane. Thus, another unit vector perpendicular to {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} is given by {tex}-\frac{\vec{a} \times \vec{b}}{|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|}=\frac{\vec{b} \times \vec{a}}{|\vec{b} \times \vec{a}|}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\vec{b} \times \vec{a}}{|\vec{b} \times \vec{a}|}=\frac{1}{3} \hat{\imath}+\frac{2}{3} \hat{\jmath}-\frac{2}{3} k{/tex} Hence, there are two unit length vectors perpendicular to the {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} .

question_text: Find the unit vector in the direction of the sum of the vectors {tex}\vec a = 2\hat i - \hat j + 2\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec b = - \hat i + \hat j + 3\hat k{/tex}
question_html: Find the unit vector in the direction of the sum of the vectors {tex}\vec a = 2\hat i - \hat j + 2\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec b = - \hat i + \hat j + 3\hat k{/tex}
solution_text: Let {tex}\vec c{/tex} denote the sum of {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} . We have {tex}\vec c = \left( {2\hat i - \hat j + 2\hat k} \right) + \left( { - \hat i + \hat j + 3\hat k} \right) = \hat i + 5\hat k{/tex} Now {tex}\left| {\vec c} \right| = \sqrt {{1^2} + {5^2}} = \sqrt {26} {/tex} Thus, the required unit vector is {tex}\vec c = \frac{{\vec c}}{{\left| {\vec c} \right|}} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt {26} }}\left( {\hat i - 5\hat k} \right) = \frac{1}{{\sqrt {26} }}\hat i + \frac{5}{{\sqrt {26} }}\hat k{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}\vec c{/tex} denote the sum of {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex}. We have

{tex}\vec c = \left( {2\hat i - \hat j + 2\hat k} \right) + \left( { - \hat i + \hat j + 3\hat k} \right) = \hat i + 5\hat k{/tex}

Now {tex}\left| {\vec c} \right| = \sqrt {{1^2} + {5^2}} = \sqrt {26} {/tex}

Thus, the required unit vector is {tex}\vec c = \frac{{\vec c}}{{\left| {\vec c} \right|}} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt {26} }}\left( {\hat i - 5\hat k} \right) = \frac{1}{{\sqrt {26} }}\hat i + \frac{5}{{\sqrt {26} }}\hat k{/tex}

question_text: Find a vector of magnitude 11 in the direction opposite to that of {tex}\overrightarrow {PQ} {/tex} , where P and Q are the points (1, 3, 2) and (-1, 0, 8) respectively.
question_html: Find a vector of magnitude 11 in the direction opposite to that of {tex}\overrightarrow {PQ} {/tex}, where P and Q are the points (1, 3, 2) and (-1, 0, 8) respectively.
solution_text: The vector with initial point P(1, 3, 2) and terminal point Q(-1, 0, 8) is given by {tex}\overrightarrow {PQ} = \left( { - 1 - 1} \right)\hat i + \left( {0 - 3} \right)\hat j + \left( {8 - 2} \right)\hat k {/tex} {tex}= - 2\hat i - 3\hat j + 6\hat k{/tex} Thus, {tex}\overrightarrow {QP} = - \overrightarrow {PQ} = 2\hat i + 3\hat j - 6\hat k{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left| {\overrightarrow {QP} } \right| = \sqrt {{2^2} + {3^2} + {{\left( { - 6} \right)}^2}} {/tex} {tex}= \sqrt {4 + 9 + 36} = \sqrt {49} = 7{/tex} Therefore, unit vector in the direction of {tex}{\overrightarrow {QP} }{/tex} is given by {tex}\hat {QP} = \frac{{\overrightarrow {QP} }}{{\left| {\overrightarrow {QP} } \right|}} = \frac{{2\hat i + 3\hat j - 6\hat k}}{7}{/tex} Hence, the required vector of magnitude 11 in direction of {tex}\overrightarrow {QP} {/tex} is 11 {tex}\overrightarrow {QP} = 11\left( {\frac{{2i + 3j - 6k}}{7}} \right) {/tex} {tex}= \frac{{22}}{7}\hat i + \frac{{33}}{7}\hat j - \frac{{66}}{7}\hat k{/tex}
solution_html:
The vector with initial point P(1, 3, 2) and terminal point Q(-1, 0, 8) is given by

{tex}\overrightarrow {PQ} = \left( { - 1 - 1} \right)\hat i + \left( {0 - 3} \right)\hat j + \left( {8 - 2} \right)\hat k {/tex} {tex}= - 2\hat i - 3\hat j + 6\hat k{/tex}

Thus, {tex}\overrightarrow {QP} = - \overrightarrow {PQ} = 2\hat i + 3\hat j - 6\hat k{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \left| {\overrightarrow {QP} } \right| = \sqrt {{2^2} + {3^2} + {{\left( { - 6} \right)}^2}} {/tex} {tex}= \sqrt {4 + 9 + 36} = \sqrt {49} = 7{/tex}

Therefore, unit vector in the direction of {tex}{\overrightarrow {QP} }{/tex} is given by

{tex}\hat {QP} = \frac{{\overrightarrow {QP} }}{{\left| {\overrightarrow {QP} } \right|}} = \frac{{2\hat i + 3\hat j - 6\hat k}}{7}{/tex}

Hence, the required vector of magnitude 11 in direction of {tex}\overrightarrow {QP} {/tex} is 11 {tex}\overrightarrow {QP} = 11\left( {\frac{{2i + 3j - 6k}}{7}} \right) {/tex} {tex}= \frac{{22}}{7}\hat i + \frac{{33}}{7}\hat j - \frac{{66}}{7}\hat k{/tex}

question_text: Find the position vector of a point R which divides the line joining the two points P and Q with position vectors {tex}\overrightarrow{O P}=2 \vec{a}+\vec{b} \text { and } \overrightarrow{O Q}=\vec{a}-2 \vec{b}{/tex} , respectively in the ratio 1 :2 internally and externally.
question_html: Find the position vector of a point R which divides the line joining the two points P and Q with position vectors {tex}\overrightarrow{O P}=2 \vec{a}+\vec{b} \text { and } \overrightarrow{O Q}=\vec{a}-2 \vec{b}{/tex}, respectively in the ratio 1 :2 internally and externally.
solution_text: Point R divides the line joining the two points $P$ and $Q$ in the ratio 1: 2 internally. Position vector of point R {tex}=\frac{1(\overrightarrow{a}-2 \overrightarrow{b})+2(2 \overrightarrow{a}+\overrightarrow{b})}{1+2}=\frac{5 \overrightarrow{a}}{3}{/tex} Point R divides the line joining the two points P and Q in the ratio 1: 2 externally Position vector of point R {tex}=\frac{1(\overrightarrow {a}-2 \overrightarrow{b})-2(2 \overrightarrow{a}+\overrightarrow{b})}{1-2}=\frac{-3 \overrightarrow{a}-4 \overrightarrow{b}}{-1}=3 \overrightarrow{a}+4 \overrightarrow{b}{/tex}
solution_html:
Point R divides the line joining the two points $P$ and $Q$ in the ratio 1: 2 internally.
Position vector of point R {tex}=\frac{1(\overrightarrow{a}-2 \overrightarrow{b})+2(2 \overrightarrow{a}+\overrightarrow{b})}{1+2}=\frac{5 \overrightarrow{a}}{3}{/tex}
Point R divides the line joining the two points P and Q in the ratio 1: 2 externally
Position vector of point R {tex}=\frac{1(\overrightarrow {a}-2 \overrightarrow{b})-2(2 \overrightarrow{a}+\overrightarrow{b})}{1-2}=\frac{-3 \overrightarrow{a}-4 \overrightarrow{b}}{-1}=3 \overrightarrow{a}+4 \overrightarrow{b}{/tex}

question_text: If the points {tex}\left( { - 1, - 1,2} \right),\left( {2,m,5} \right){/tex} and {tex}\left( {3,11,6} \right){/tex} are collinear, find the value of m.
question_html: If the points {tex}\left( { - 1, - 1,2} \right),\left( {2,m,5} \right){/tex} and {tex}\left( {3,11,6} \right){/tex} are collinear, find the value of m.
solution_text: Let the given points be A(-1, -1, 2), B(2, m, 5) and {tex}C\left( {3,11,6} \right){/tex} . Then {tex}\overrightarrow {AB} = \left( {2 + 1} \right)\hat i + \left( {m + 1} \right)\hat j + \left( {5 - 2} \right)\hat k {/tex} {tex}= 3\hat i + \left( {m + 1} \right)\hat j + 3\hat k{/tex} And {tex}\overrightarrow {AC} = \left( {3 + 1} \right)\hat i + \left( {11 + 1} \right)\hat j + \left( {6 - 2} \right)\hat k {/tex} {tex}= 4\hat i + 12\hat j + 4\hat k{/tex} Since A, B, C are collinear, we have {tex}\overrightarrow {AB} = \lambda \overrightarrow {AC} {/tex} i.e., {tex}\left( {3\hat i + \left( {m + 1} \right)\hat j + 3\hat k} \right) = \lambda \left( {4\hat i + 12\hat j + 4\hat k} \right){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 3 = 4\lambda {/tex} and {tex}m + 1 = 12\lambda {/tex} (solving equations) Therefore, m = 8.
solution_html:
Let the given points be A(-1, -1, 2), B(2, m, 5) and {tex}C\left( {3,11,6} \right){/tex}. Then {tex}\overrightarrow {AB} = \left( {2 + 1} \right)\hat i + \left( {m + 1} \right)\hat j + \left( {5 - 2} \right)\hat k {/tex} {tex}= 3\hat i + \left( {m + 1} \right)\hat j + 3\hat k{/tex}

And {tex}\overrightarrow {AC} = \left( {3 + 1} \right)\hat i + \left( {11 + 1} \right)\hat j + \left( {6 - 2} \right)\hat k {/tex} {tex}= 4\hat i + 12\hat j + 4\hat k{/tex}

Since A, B, C are collinear, we have {tex}\overrightarrow {AB} = \lambda \overrightarrow {AC} {/tex} i.e.,

{tex}\left( {3\hat i + \left( {m + 1} \right)\hat j + 3\hat k} \right) = \lambda \left( {4\hat i + 12\hat j + 4\hat k} \right){/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow 3 = 4\lambda {/tex} and {tex}m + 1 = 12\lambda {/tex} (solving equations)

Therefore, m = 8.

question_text: Find a vector {tex}\vec r{/tex} of magnitude {tex}3\sqrt 2 {/tex} units which makes an angle of {tex}\frac{\pi }{4}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{\pi }{2}{/tex} with y and z - axes, respectively.
question_html: Find a vector {tex}\vec r{/tex} of magnitude {tex}3\sqrt 2 {/tex} units which makes an angle of {tex}\frac{\pi }{4}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{\pi }{2}{/tex} with y and z - axes, respectively.
solution_text: Here {tex}m = \cos \frac{\pi }{4} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}{/tex} and {tex}n = \cos \frac{\pi }{2} = 0{/tex} Therefore, l 2 + m 2 + n 2 = 1 gives {tex}{l^2} + \frac{1}{2} + 0 = 1{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow l = \pm \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}{/tex} Hence, the required vector {tex}\vec r = 3\sqrt 2 \left( {l\hat i + m\hat j + n\hat k} \right){/tex} is given by {tex}\vec r = 3\sqrt 2 \left( { \pm \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}\hat i + \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}\hat j + 0\hat k} \right) = \vec r = \pm 3\hat i + 3\hat j.{/tex}
solution_html:
Here {tex}m = \cos \frac{\pi }{4} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}{/tex} and {tex}n = \cos \frac{\pi }{2} = 0{/tex}
Therefore, l2 + m2 + n2 = 1 gives
{tex}{l^2} + \frac{1}{2} + 0 = 1{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow l = \pm \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}{/tex}
Hence, the required vector {tex}\vec r = 3\sqrt 2 \left( {l\hat i + m\hat j + n\hat k} \right){/tex} is given by
{tex}\vec r = 3\sqrt 2 \left( { \pm \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}\hat i + \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}\hat j + 0\hat k} \right) = \vec r = \pm 3\hat i + 3\hat j.{/tex}

book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: If {tex}\vec a = 2\hat i - \hat j + \hat k,\vec b = \hat i + \hat j - 2\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec c = \hat i + 3\hat j - \hat k{/tex} find {tex}\lambda {/tex} such that {tex}\vec a{/tex} is perpendicular to {tex}\lambda \vec b + \vec c{/tex}
question_html: If {tex}\vec a = 2\hat i - \hat j + \hat k,\vec b = \hat i + \hat j - 2\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec c = \hat i + 3\hat j - \hat k{/tex} find {tex}\lambda {/tex} such that {tex}\vec a{/tex} is perpendicular to {tex}\lambda \vec b + \vec c{/tex}
solution_text: We have {tex}\lambda \vec b + \vec c = \lambda \left( {\hat i + \hat j - 2\hat k} \right) + \left( {\hat i + 3\hat j - \hat k} \right){/tex} {tex}= \left( {\lambda +1} \right)\hat i + \left( {\lambda + 3} \right)\hat j - \left( {2\lambda + 1} \right)\hat k{/tex} Since {tex}\vec a \bot \left( {\lambda \vec b + \vec c} \right),\vec a.\left( {\lambda \vec b + \vec c} \right) = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \left( {2\hat i - \hat j + \hat k} \right).\left[ {\left( {\lambda + 1} \right)\hat i + \left( {\lambda + 3} \right)\hat j - \left( {2\lambda + 1} \right)\hat k} \right] = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2\left( {\lambda + 1} \right) - \left( {\lambda + 3} \right) - \left( {2\lambda + 1} \right) = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \lambda = - 2{/tex}
solution_html:
We have

{tex}\lambda \vec b + \vec c = \lambda \left( {\hat i + \hat j - 2\hat k} \right) + \left( {\hat i + 3\hat j - \hat k} \right){/tex}

{tex}= \left( {\lambda +1} \right)\hat i + \left( {\lambda + 3} \right)\hat j - \left( {2\lambda + 1} \right)\hat k{/tex}

Since {tex}\vec a \bot \left( {\lambda \vec b + \vec c} \right),\vec a.\left( {\lambda \vec b + \vec c} \right) = 0{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow \left( {2\hat i - \hat j + \hat k} \right).\left[ {\left( {\lambda + 1} \right)\hat i + \left( {\lambda + 3} \right)\hat j - \left( {2\lambda + 1} \right)\hat k} \right] = 0{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow 2\left( {\lambda + 1} \right) - \left( {\lambda + 3} \right) - \left( {2\lambda + 1} \right) = 0{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow \lambda = - 2{/tex}

question_text: Find all vectors of magnitude {tex}10 \sqrt{3}{/tex} that are perpendicular to the plane of {tex}\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}-\hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}{/tex}
question_html: Find all vectors of magnitude {tex}10 \sqrt{3}{/tex} that are perpendicular to the plane of {tex}\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}-\hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}{/tex}
solution_text: Let {tex}\vec{a}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}=-\hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}{/tex} . Then {tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}{/tex} = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} {\hat{i}} & {\hat{j}} & {\hat{k}} \\ {1} & {2} & {1} \\ {-1} & {3} & {4} \end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\hat i{/tex} (8 + 3) - {tex}\hat j{/tex} (4 + 1) + {tex}\hat k{/tex} (3 + 2) = 5 {tex}\hat i{/tex} - 5 {tex}\hat j{/tex} + 5 {tex}\hat k{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow |\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|=\sqrt{(5)^{2}+(-5)^{2}+(5)^{2}}=\sqrt{3(5)^{2}}=5 \sqrt{3}{/tex} . Therefore, unit vector perpendicular to the plane of {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} is given by {tex}\frac{\vec{a} \times \vec{b}}{|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|}=\frac{5 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k}}{5 \sqrt{3}}{/tex} Hence, vectors of the magnitude of {tex}10 \sqrt3{/tex} that are perpendicular to plane of {tex}\vec a {/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} are {tex}\pm 10 \sqrt{3}\left(\frac{5 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k}}{5 \sqrt{3}}\right){/tex} , i.e., {tex}\pm 10(\hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}){/tex} .
solution_html:
Let {tex}\vec{a}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}=-\hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}{/tex}. Then 
{tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}{/tex} = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} {\hat{i}} & {\hat{j}} & {\hat{k}} \\ {1} & {2} & {1} \\ {-1} & {3} & {4} \end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\hat i{/tex}(8 + 3) - {tex}\hat j{/tex}(4 + 1) + {tex}\hat k{/tex}(3 + 2) = 5{tex}\hat i{/tex} - 5{tex}\hat j{/tex} + 5{tex}\hat k{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow |\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|=\sqrt{(5)^{2}+(-5)^{2}+(5)^{2}}=\sqrt{3(5)^{2}}=5 \sqrt{3}{/tex}.
Therefore, unit vector perpendicular to the plane of {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} is given by
{tex}\frac{\vec{a} \times \vec{b}}{|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|}=\frac{5 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k}}{5 \sqrt{3}}{/tex}
Hence, vectors of the magnitude of {tex}10 \sqrt3{/tex} that are perpendicular to plane of {tex}\vec a {/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} are {tex}\pm 10 \sqrt{3}\left(\frac{5 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k}}{5 \sqrt{3}}\right){/tex}, i.e., {tex}\pm 10(\hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}){/tex}.

question_text: Using vectors, prove that cos (A - B) = cosA cosB + sinA sinB.
question_html: Using vectors, prove that cos (A - B) = cosA cosB + sinA sinB.
solution_text: Let {tex}\hat{\mathrm{OP}}{/tex} and {tex}\hat{\mathrm{OQ}}{/tex} be unit vectors making angles A and B, respectively, with positive direction of x-axis. Then {tex}\angle QOP{/tex} = A - B We know {tex}\hat{\mathrm{OP}}{/tex} = {tex}\vec{\mathrm{OM}}+\vec{\mathrm{MP}}{/tex} = {tex}\hat{i} \cos \mathrm{A}+\hat{j} \sin \mathrm{A}{/tex} and {tex}\hat{\mathrm{OQ}}=\vec{\mathrm{ON}}+\vec{\mathrm{NQ}}=\hat{i} \cos \mathrm{B}+\hat{j} \sin \mathrm{B}{/tex} . By definition {tex}\hat{\mathrm{OP}} \cdot \hat{\mathrm{OQ}}=|\hat{\mathrm{OP}}||\hat{\mathrm{OQ}}| \cos (\mathrm{A}-\mathrm{B}){/tex} = cos (A - B) ...(1) {tex}(\cdot \cdot|\hat{\mathrm{OP}}|=1|\hat{\mathrm{OQ}}|){/tex} In terms of components, we have {tex}\hat{\mathrm{OP}} \cdot \hat{\mathrm{OQ}}=(\hat{i} \cos \mathrm{A}+\hat{j} \sin \mathrm{A}) \cdot(\hat{i} \cos \mathrm{B}+\hat{j} \sin \mathrm{B}){/tex} = cosA cosB + sinA sinB ... (2) From (1) and (2), we get cos (A - B) = cosA cosB + sinA sinB.
solution_html:
Let {tex}\hat{\mathrm{OP}}{/tex} and {tex}\hat{\mathrm{OQ}}{/tex} be unit vectors making angles A and B, respectively, with positive direction of x-axis. Then {tex}\angle QOP{/tex} = A - B

We know {tex}\hat{\mathrm{OP}}{/tex} = {tex}\vec{\mathrm{OM}}+\vec{\mathrm{MP}}{/tex} = {tex}\hat{i} \cos \mathrm{A}+\hat{j} \sin \mathrm{A}{/tex} and {tex}\hat{\mathrm{OQ}}=\vec{\mathrm{ON}}+\vec{\mathrm{NQ}}=\hat{i} \cos \mathrm{B}+\hat{j} \sin \mathrm{B}{/tex}.
By definition {tex}\hat{\mathrm{OP}} \cdot \hat{\mathrm{OQ}}=|\hat{\mathrm{OP}}||\hat{\mathrm{OQ}}| \cos (\mathrm{A}-\mathrm{B}){/tex} 
= cos (A - B) ...(1) {tex}(\cdot \cdot|\hat{\mathrm{OP}}|=1|\hat{\mathrm{OQ}}|){/tex}
In terms of components, we have
{tex}\hat{\mathrm{OP}} \cdot \hat{\mathrm{OQ}}=(\hat{i} \cos \mathrm{A}+\hat{j} \sin \mathrm{A}) \cdot(\hat{i} \cos \mathrm{B}+\hat{j} \sin \mathrm{B}){/tex} 
= cosA cosB + sinA sinB ... (2)
From (1) and (2), we get
cos (A - B) = cosA cosB + sinA sinB.
