question_text: Prove that in a {tex}\triangle ABC,\frac{{\sin A}}{a} = \frac{{\sin B}}{b} = \frac{{\sin C}}{c}{/tex} where a, b, c represents the magnitude of the sides opposite to vertices A, B, C respectively.
question_html: Prove that in a {tex}\triangle ABC,\frac{{\sin A}}{a} = \frac{{\sin B}}{b} = \frac{{\sin C}}{c}{/tex} where a, b, c represents the magnitude of the sides opposite to vertices A, B, C respectively.
solution_text: Let the three sides of the triangle BC, CA and AB be represented by {tex}\vec a,\vec b{/tex} and {tex}\vec c{/tex} , respectively in the figure. We have {tex}\vec a + \vec b + \vec c = \vec 0.{/tex} i.e., {tex}\vec a + \vec b = - \vec c{/tex} Which pre-cross multiplying by {tex}\vec a{/tex} , and post cross multiplying by {tex}\vec b{/tex} , given {tex}\vec a \times \vec b = \vec c \times \vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec a \times \vec b = \vec b \times \vec c{/tex} respectively. Therefore, {tex}\vec a \times \vec b = \vec b \times \vec c = \vec c \times \vec a{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left| {\vec a \times \vec b} \right|\left| {\vec b \times \vec c} \right|\left| {\vec c \times \vec b} \right|{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left| {\vec a} \right|\left| {\vec b} \right|\sin \left( {\pi - C} \right) = \left| {\vec b} \right|\left| {\vec c} \right|\sin \left( {\pi - A} \right)\left| {\vec c} \right|\left| {\vec a} \right|\sin \left( {\pi - B} \right){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow ab\sin C = bc\sin A = ca\sin B{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left| {\vec a \times \vec b} \right| = \left| {\vec b \times \vec c} \right| = \left| {\vec c \times \vec b} \right|{/tex} Dividing by abc, we get {tex}\frac{{\sin C}}{c} = \frac{{\sin A}}{a} = \frac{{\sin B}}{b}{/tex} {tex}i.e,\,\frac{{\sin A}}{a} = \frac{{\sin B}}{b} = \frac{{\sin C}}{c}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let the three sides of the triangle BC, CA and AB be represented by {tex}\vec a,\vec b{/tex} and {tex}\vec c{/tex}, respectively in the figure.

We have {tex}\vec a + \vec b + \vec c = \vec 0.{/tex} i.e., {tex}\vec a + \vec b = - \vec c{/tex} 
Which pre-cross multiplying by {tex}\vec a{/tex}, and post cross multiplying by {tex}\vec b{/tex}, given {tex}\vec a \times \vec b = \vec c \times \vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec a \times \vec b = \vec b \times \vec c{/tex} respectively. Therefore,
{tex}\vec a \times \vec b = \vec b \times \vec c = \vec c \times \vec a{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \left| {\vec a \times \vec b} \right|\left| {\vec b \times \vec c} \right|\left| {\vec c \times \vec b} \right|{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \left| {\vec a} \right|\left| {\vec b} \right|\sin \left( {\pi - C} \right) = \left| {\vec b} \right|\left| {\vec c} \right|\sin \left( {\pi - A} \right)\left| {\vec c} \right|\left| {\vec a} \right|\sin \left( {\pi - B} \right){/tex} 
{tex} \Rightarrow ab\sin C = bc\sin A = ca\sin B{/tex}  {tex}\Rightarrow \left| {\vec a \times \vec b} \right| = \left| {\vec b \times \vec c} \right| = \left| {\vec c \times \vec b} \right|{/tex} 
Dividing by abc, we get
{tex}\frac{{\sin C}}{c} = \frac{{\sin A}}{a} = \frac{{\sin B}}{b}{/tex} {tex}i.e,\,\frac{{\sin A}}{a} = \frac{{\sin B}}{b} = \frac{{\sin C}}{c}{/tex}

question_html: The 2 vectors {tex}\hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}3 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+4 \hat{k}{/tex} represents the two sides AB and AC, respectively of a {tex} \triangle{/tex}ABC. The length of the median through A is

options.explanation: In {tex}\triangle ABC,{/tex} Using the triangle law of vector addition, we have {tex}\overrightarrow{B C}=\overrightarrow{A C}-\overrightarrow{A B}{/tex} {tex}=(3 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+4 \hat{k})-(\hat{j}+\hat{k}){/tex} {tex}\therefore \overrightarrow{B D}=\frac{1}{2} \overrightarrow{B C}=\frac{3}{2} \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\frac{3}{2} \hat{k}{/tex} (since AD is the median) In {tex}\triangle \mathrm{ABD},{/tex} using the triangle law of vector addition, we have {tex}\overrightarrow{A D}=\overrightarrow{A B}+\overrightarrow{B D}{/tex} {tex}=(\hat{j}+\hat{k})+\left(\frac{3}{2} \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\frac{3}{2} \hat{k}\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{3}{2} \hat{i}+0 \hat{j}+\frac{5}{2} \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\therefore A D=\sqrt{\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^{2}+0^{2}+\left(\frac{5}{2}\right)^{2}}=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{34}{/tex} Hence, the length of the median through A is {tex}\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{34} \text { units. }{/tex}

options.explanation: [0, 6] is the correct answer. The smallest value of {tex}|k \vec{a}|{/tex} will exist at numerically smallest value of k, i.e., at k = 0, which gives {tex}|k \vec{a}|=|k||\vec{a}|=0 \times 3=0{/tex} The numerically greatest value of k is 2 at which {tex}|k \vec{a}|{/tex} = 6.
